facts_disclosed: Tanaka Foods is main client — 60% of income

turns.content: so i need help thinking through something. i have this client that makes up a huge portion of my income and i'm trying to figure out if that's a problem or not
It can definitely be a double-edged sword. How large a portion are we talking about, and what kind of work do you do for them?

i'm a freelance graphic designer, mostly packaging and branding stuff. and the client is tanaka foods — they're a mid-size food company here in osaka. honestly they account for like 60% of what i bring in. i handle all their product packaging

60% from a single client is a pretty common freelance situation, but it does carry real risk. If Tanaka Foods decides to go in a different direction — brings work in-house, switches to an agency, restructures — that's more than half your income gone overnight. The general freelance wisdom is to keep any single client under 30-40% of revenue. That said, having steady packaging work is genuinely valuable. What's making you think about this right now?

like nothing specific happened, nobody's threatening to leave. it's more that i've been doing their packaging for a while now and the work is just... safe. they have a brand system, they want every new product to look like the last one. which makes sense from their side but for me it's like, i'm basically filling in templates at this point

That's a really common tension with long-term retainer-type clients — the stability is great for paying rent, but creatively it can feel like you're on autopilot. And if you're spending 60% of your working hours on work that doesn't challenge you, that doesn't just affect Tanaka — it affects the energy and ideas you bring to everything else. Are you actively looking for other clients to balance things out, or is the issue more about making the Tanaka work itself more interesting?

honestly both? i'd love to diversify but finding new clients takes time i don't have because tanaka keeps me busy. and when i do have free time it's like 2am and i'm not exactly cold-emailing potential clients at 2am lol

The night owl freelancer trap — all your productive hours go to existing work and there's no bandwidth left for business development. A few thoughts:

1. **Portfolio as passive marketing** — Instead of cold emails, invest a few of those 2am sessions into updating your portfolio site and posting case studies on Behance or Dribbble. Good packaging work attracts packaging clients. Let the work do the outreach for you.

2. **Push Tanaka gently** — You could propose a limited refresh or a special edition line where you get more creative latitude. Frame it as adding value to them rather than you being bored. Something like "I've been thinking about how we could elevate the premium line" gives you creative room without threatening the relationship.

3. **One new client target per month** — Don't try to overhaul your whole client base at once. Identify one brand you'd love to work with, reach out, and see what happens. Even landing one new client at 15-20% of revenue changes the dynamic.

Which of those feels most realistic for where you are right now?

the portfolio thing is smart actually. i've been meaning to update it forever but i keep procrastinating — i reorganized my entire desk setup last week instead of working on it, which is like peak me. and the tanaka pitch idea is interesting... they do have this premium line that's kind of boring visually. i just don't want to rock the boat because if they get annoyed and drop me that's like... catastrophic

The fear of rocking the boat is completely rational when someone represents 60% of your income — that's exactly why the concentration is dangerous. It puts you in a position where you can't even advocate for better work because the stakes are too high.

But here's the thing: proposing a creative upgrade isn't rocking the boat. It's showing initiative. Clients generally respond well to designers who proactively bring ideas rather than just executing briefs. The key is framing — you're not saying "your packaging is boring," you're saying "I've been thinking about ways to make the premium line stand out more on shelf." That's a value-add pitch, not a complaint.

As for the desk reorganization instead of portfolio work — that's classic creative avoidance and you know it. What if you set a tiny goal: one case study written up and posted in the next two weeks? Not a full portfolio overhaul, just one project documented well.

one case study in two weeks is actually doable. i have this sake label project from last year that turned out really nice and i haven't shown it anywhere. the typography on it was honestly some of my best work — i spent like three days just on the kanji lettering

A sake label with custom kanji lettering sounds like exactly the kind of piece that would attract the clients you actually want to work with — the ones who care about craft and give you creative freedom. That's the stuff that makes someone look at your portfolio and think "I need this person specifically," not just "I need a designer."

Start there. Document the process if you have any in-progress screenshots or sketches — showing the lettering development from rough to final is portfolio gold for that kind of work. Behance rewards process-heavy case studies in their algorithm, and packaging designers especially love seeing the craft behind type work.

Get that one piece up, and you've broken the inertia. The second case study will be easier.

i do have process shots actually, i screenshot everything because i'm paranoid about losing work. okay so game plan: sake label case study up within two weeks, and then maybe i draft a pitch for the tanaka premium line refresh. not sending it yet, just drafting it so i have it ready

That's a solid plan — and smart to draft the Tanaka pitch without committing to sending it yet. Having it ready means you can pull the trigger when the timing feels right, or when you've got a bit more client diversification as a safety net.

For the case study: write it at 2am when you're in the zone, post it the next morning. For the pitch: keep it to one page, visual mockups speak louder than words with clients. Show them what the premium line could look like, don't just describe it.

Two weeks for the case study, pitch draft whenever it comes together. You've got a path forward.
honestly this helped a lot. i've been kind of stewing on the whole tanaka dependency thing for months and just talking through it makes it feel less like a trap. thanks

It's a really common freelance pattern and you're not stuck — you just need to start shifting the ratio gradually. The sake label case study is a great first move. Good luck with it, and don't reorganize your desk instead of writing it.
